text: In this video we will demonstrate technical aspects of robotic assisted left upper lobectomy for squamous cell carcinoma.
The video shows the robotic-assisted technique used to navigate and manipulate the challenging anatomy of the upper pulmonary artery branches during a left upper lobectomy.
A sixty-one year old male initially presented with dyspnea on exertion and was found to have a two point six centimeters left upper lob mass. On biopsy, this was found to be squamous cell carcinoma.
His PET-CT and IBIS were negative for lymph node disease. Therefore, he was staged T-I-C-N-Zero-M-Zero clinical stage I-A.
This is his imaging showing the left upper lobe speculated perihelion mass. PET-CT showing SUV uptake.

The patient is positioned in the right lateral decubitus position with arms suspended, and the surgical team demonstrates the placement of robotic ports on the left side of the thorax for the procedure.

Four 8mm robotic ports are placed in the eighth intercostal space along the midclavicular, anterior axillary, posterior axillary, and perispinal lines, providing access for the robotic-assisted pulmonary lobectomy.
An additional 15mm assistant port is placed at the subcostal margin along the anterior axillary line, complementing the existing robotic ports for the pulmonary lobectomy procedure.
The surgeon begins the dissection by using bipolar cautery to take down the inferior pulmonary ligament. A level nine lymph node is identified and carefully dissected out from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon identifies and dissects out a level seven lymph node pocket. The anterior oblique fissure is divided using a pulper staple load, complemented by gentle blunt dissection.
The surgeon identifies and dissects out a level XI lymph node located within the oblique fissure. Above the node pocket, a clear passage is visible from the interlobar fissure to the posterior hilum.

The surgeon identifies the inferior lingual branch of the pulmonary artery within the interlobar fissure, circumferentially controls it using blunt dissection, and divides it. Subsequently, the superior lingual artery is also divided.

The bedside assistant introduces a wide load stapler through the posterior port. The surgeon completes the division of the posterior fissure using purple staple loads inserted through the anterior port.
The surgeon identifies the apico-posterior branch of the pulmonary artery, circumferentially controls it, and divides it within the surgical field.

The surgeon retracts the upper lobe posteriorly to isolate and divide the superior pulmonary vein, as the anatomical features prevent identification of the anterior branch of the pulmonary artery at this stage.
The surgeon divides the left upper lobe bronchus using electrocautery, providing clear exposure of the anterior branch of the pulmonary artery for safe identification and division.
The surgeon closes the left upper bronchial stem using a running IIIO absorbable V-lock suture, securing the bronchial stump with precise stitching.
The surgeon places the resected left upper lobe into a specimen retrieval bag and removes it through the previously made incision and the 15mm assistant port.

After the specimen removal, the surgeon performs a multilevel intercostal block and places a single 28 French chest tube through the camera port incision. The lung is inflated, and the remaining surgical wounds are closed.

This patient's post-operative course was complicated by COVID-Nineteen infection, respiratory failure and prolonged air leak. He was intubated with subsequent tracheostomy and prolonged hospital stay. He was discharged to the nursing facility on post-operative day number forty-four. However, this complication was not related to the surgical technique itself.

The surgeon uses fully articulated instruments to perform precise dissection around the unusual branch of the anterior segmental artery, avoiding vessel injuries and other potential intraoperative mistakes.
